item.title: 11 points?
item.content:encoded: Honestly felt like this article is pretty well written. Unsure if it's because previous versions were lackluster or what, but this is a +1 from me.

item.title: Re: Update# 1
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3944 causes a "loop" of interrupted thought processes.Instances of SCP-3944-1

Needs a space after this sentence.

item.title: Update# 1
item.content:encoded: Due to the critiques received, I made some minor edits to the description section, final test and removed the Incidence Log. These changes have not altered article in a significant way other then improving read ability and reducing extraneous length.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay cool. I figured as much, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm unclear on the rules for making major edits after its been posted.

You can edit your own article however you want, whenever you want. Readers that didn't like it the first time usually don't come back, though - if you're going to majorly overhaul a negative-rated article, you're probably better off taking it down and making all your improvements in a draft before republishing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this 'number (#)' thing some aspect of clinical tone that I don't know about?

In real life, it's done so that if the word is misspelled then the number can be there to clear up any ambiguity. It's mostly legal stuff, so someone can't say they messed up because they couldn't read the number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the input. I have been trying to figure out what can be done to help make it better. But I'm unclear on the rules for making major edits after its been posted. I tried getting as much feed back as possible, unfortunately I was plagued by new members with opinions or no one being interested in taking the time. A few people helped immensely and to be honest they helped it became what it is now. But I was left alone for a lot of it.

Another issue I had was a degree of conflicting advice. I personally felt my article was getting too long, but people wanted more detail (particularly the test logs), and others wanted it shorter. I tried my best to achieve what was requested while maintaining the core of it all. It's my first attempt at writing any fiction actually and I didn't have the highest expectations, its been fun watching the rating go up then down and back again. And Ill be the first to admit this is really hard lol. But its really fun and I want to get better.

And as for the concept, sort of? It was born from personal experience in a way. I tend to forget my point or what I am talking about in heated discussion and sometimes for the life of me can't remember. A lot of time this happened while hanging out with friends in a backyard somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is interesting for a first scip, but quite honestly I wasn't feeling it. Altogether I think there could have been further adjustments. The biggest issue I have with this scip is that it hasn't been tightened to fit a clinical tone. That's vague so I'll explain what I mean by that.

crit, click me!
close

Description: SCP-3944 is an outdoor patio table from ███████. The object is a iron round table, matte black in color, approximately 80cm in height with a radius of 61cm, with a surface being made of 5mm tempered glass.

Right away, this description has some redundant information. Clinical tone is concerned with displaying information in a way that's easy to understand while still remaining practical. This first paragraph could easily be shortened to a single sentence to achieve that end:

"SCP-3944 is a black iron patio table measuring 80 cm in height and 122 cm in width, with a 5 mm thick tempered glass surface."

SCP-3944 causes a "loop" of interrupted thought processes. It is expressed as an instance of SCP-3944-1 stopping mid conversation to ask the instance to their immediate left if they can recall the topic of conversation, having a momentary lapse in train of thought mid-sentence. It is at this point a repetitive looping effect takes place. In a steady and consistent, sequential clockwise order, instances of SCP-3944-1 will begin repeating a series of questions and replies to each other. This will not stop until either an instance is removed from SCP-3944's radius, causing the number of subjects to fall below the minimum requirement for activation.

Honestly, I had to reread this paragraph a couple times to understand what it was trying to describe. So, when people sit at the table, they become influenced by the mind-affecting properties, becoming SCP-3944-1. After this, their memory and attention span become shortened, and they're unable to leave the table. I think you could manage to cut out a bit of extraneous explanation here to make it more easily understood.

I will say that I think this scip suffers from a lack of critiques. You did get one critique, which is good, but you should look into getting even more eyes on a work before posting it. Also, try to prioritize getting crits from members that have written successful articles, because they may have a better idea for the flow of things.

Other than the issues I had with the description, I found the dialogue in the incident log to be a bit stilted, and it didn't read like real people talking. I suggest reading the dialogue out loud to gauge how you could change it to sound less awkward and more real.

I also found all but the final testing log to be unnecessary. I'm more interested in the greater meaning behind these mind-affecting properties, not how the Foundation came to understand them. You did attempt to tell a greater story in the final testing log, but it didn't feel like it led anywhere, nor did it evoke an emotional reaction from me.

Final thoughts: this is a play on a "round table" discussion, isn't it?

It's not a bad concept, mind you, I just think it should have gone through few more crits to shape it into something great before posting. So unfortunately that's a downvote from me. Good luck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept has potential, and definitely reminded me of the unsettling nature of some Series 1 articles. The dialogue feels very hackneyed ("Fascinating!"), and makes the article suffer for it, especially in parts like the Recovery (which I agree is extraneous and unneeded, as Shaggy said). I also don't see the motive for the Site Director to go through with an unethical test. Knowing that the table is more "mind-controlling" than mind-affecting does add a bit, but I see no motivation in forcing random D-class to endure multiple years of torture for minimal returns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I couldn't make it past the first two addenda, which really only contained bare exposition in trying to exhaustively detail every single step of the process leading up to containment. This isn't always a bad thing, if there were something really intruiging or compelling about this part of the story, but here it causes the progression of the article to come to a screeching halt.

I'm personally pefectly fine with accepting that something is in containment without knowing how they discovered and contained it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow thank you for your positive and well written review! I spent a lot of time on it and it’s actually my first work of fiction I have ever shared pubilcally. Unfortunately I’m not much of a writer lol. I did my best on the grammar, but aside from Word or people pointing things out, I can’t really tell.

If there is anything I can fix, don’t hesitate to highlight it. Ultimately I want people to like it, and people here are sticklers for grammar. As they should be .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow! Excellent work for a first article! There are a few minor errors involving grammar, but nothing that would obscure readability or detract from the article in its entirety. It's a well-executed, unique concept that I haven't seen or read before.

I was actually interested in reading this article so that I could support newer authors like yourself, and after reading the description and Discovery Log I was somewhat intrigued. When you really piqued my interest, however, was when you began the test logs. The final two test logs were very compelling reads!

All in all, for a first article, well done! Keep up the creativity and I'm looking forward to more from you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know what I had been struggling with the amended number thing, and you are the first person to bring it up, Ill change it if I can. Thanks for the vote. And omg of course one got through.

item.title: Here Goes Nothing
item.content:encoded:
Whelp here it is, my first SCP article. If there are any glaring problems please let me know, as stated I have never posted to the main list before. Big shout out to user: bilaros for all the help in the critiques forum.

Image credit: Roger Mommaerts
Attribution-ShareAlike 2.0 Generic (CC BY-SA 2.0)
Image link: https://www.flickr.com/photos/rmommaerts/3484603754/in/photostream/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Transportation of SCP-3944 should be carried out by no more than three (3) individuals, though typically two (2) will be adequate.

I don't understand why people do this. Is this 'number (#)' thing some aspect of clinical tone that I don't know about? Because it really just seems like people do this because a lot of early, classic anomalies do it. I don't get it, nor do I really like it, especially since it's not done consistently. Some numbers have their numeral amended onto it and some don't. It's just… why? It breaks up the flow of the article in a weird way for me.

But that's kind of a minor gripe. As for the anomaly itself… I kinda like it. The pace of the article did a good job of holding my attention all the way to the end. The anomaly and the way it was presented was weird, in a good way. It was original. I haven't seen anything quite like it, at least. Once I had a clear understanding of what the anomaly actually did, I wasn't really sure how to feel about it, but the progressively escalating tests really held it together for me and kept me invested until the end.

I like it enough to upvote. The only gripes I really have with this are the 'number (#)' thing, and one SCP-XXXX that was left behind at the end of Test Log 01. Other than that, good job, me likey.
